description: Derivation Concepts with mathematical naming AND explicit relationships to dimensional concepts. Same dimensional relationship structure as Option D, but using mathematical concept names.
designRationale: In v0.20a and v0.20b, dimensional concepts are listed as available but the relationship between a derivation concept and its dimensions is left to the Transformation. This means every Transformation repeats the same dimensional bindings (Subject, Parameter, Visit) even when they are structurally determined by the concept. In v0.20c, the concept carries these relationships, so the Transformation only declares what the concept does not already know.

dimensionalConcepts.note: These are concepts in the model, but they are not derivation concepts. They define the context in which a derivation is performed. The Implementation maps them to physical columns (e.g. USUBJID → Subject, PARAMCD → Parameter, AVISIT → Visit).

Subject.definition: The individual for whom the derivation is computed.

Parameter.definition: The measure or endpoint being derived.

dimensionalRoles.note: When a concept declares a relationship to a dimensional concept, it specifies the role that dimension plays in the derivation.
dimensionalRoles.partition: The dimension defines a group. The derivation is computed independently within each partition. The dimension is preserved in the output.
dimensionalRoles.constraint: The dimension imposes a structural rule. For example, Comparison requires that both inputs share the same Parameter.
dimensionalRoles.order: The dimension provides the ordering axis. The derivation summarises across this axis — it collapses in the output.
dimensionalRoles.scope: The dimension defines the boundary within which the derivation operates, without partitioning or ordering.

PointComputation.definition: A value computed at a specific point, without reference comparison, temporal aggregation, event aggregation, or classification.
PointComputation.inputPattern: Method-driven. The concept defines output shape only; the method determines what inputs are needed.

Parameter.note: Each input and output Measure has a Parameter. Inputs may have different parameters (e.g. WEIGHT, HEIGHT for BMI), and the output may create a new one (BMI).

DirectValue.definition: A derived value produced by applying a method to one or more source observations or derived measures.

Comparison.definition: Data expressing the relationship between two values of the same parameter.
Comparison.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 2× DirectValue inputs, intrinsic to the category.

Parameter.note: Both inputs must share the same parameter. The output inherits it.

Timing.note: The two inputs exist at different time points; the output sits at the source timepoint.

AbsoluteDifference.definition: Arithmetic difference between two values of the same parameter.

RelativeDifference.definition: Proportional difference between two values of the same parameter, relative to the reference.
examples: percent change from baseline in tumor size

Ratio.definition: Quotient of two values of the same parameter.

LogRatio.definition: Logarithm of the quotient of two values. Symmetric, additive scale.

CategoricalTransition.definition: Categorical movement between two values of the same parameter.

SequenceAggregate.definition: A summary of an ordered series of the same parameter over time.
SequenceAggregate.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× DirectValue input; the ordered series emerges from dimensional scoping.

Parameter.note: The series is of a single parameter. Inherited by the output.

Timing.note: Provides the ordering axis. COLLAPSES in output — the summary is across all timepoints.

Period.note: May define the window over which the series is summarised.

SequenceMaximum.definition: Maximum value in an ordered sequence.

SequenceMinimum.definition: Minimum value in an ordered sequence.

SequenceArgMaximum.definition: Time at which maximum value occurs.

SequenceArgMinimum.definition: Time at which minimum value occurs.

SequenceIntegral.definition: Integrated value over time (area under curve).

SequenceRateOfChange.definition: Rate of change over a segment (slope).

ExponentialDecayConstant.definition: Time for value to decline by 50%.

SequenceTerminalValue.definition: Last observed value in the sequence.

SequenceThresholdDuration.definition: Total time spent above or below a threshold.

SequenceThresholdCrossing.definition: Time at which sequence first crosses a threshold.

SequenceRange.definition: Difference between maximum and minimum in sequence.

EventAggregate.definition: A summary of discrete event occurrences on a timeline.
EventAggregate.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× Observation (OC layer); the event set emerges from dimensional scoping.

Parameter.note: The event type (e.g. specific AE term). Output typically gets a new parameter.

Timing.note: Provides the timeline over which events are counted. Not an ordering axis — events are discrete occurrences.

Period.note: May define the window in which events are counted.

Count.definition: Number of occurrences of an event or condition.

Rate.definition: Number of occurrences per unit of time or exposure.

TimeToEvent.definition: Duration from a defined start to event occurrence or censoring.

Classification.definition: A derivation mapping value(s) to a categorical assignment.
Classification.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× any concept as input (the value being classified).

Parameter.note: The classification may be parameter-specific (e.g. responder flag per parameter) or create a new parameter (e.g. population flag).

Timing.note: Preserved when classifying at each timepoint.

Rank.definition: Ordinal position of value within a defined scope.

Quantile.math: Qₖ where F(x) in bin k

transformationExample_v020c.note: Same structure as Option D but with mathematical concept names.

T.ChangeFromBaseline.note: The Transformation only adds AnalysisVisit. Subject and Parameter (with the same-parameter constraint) are inherited from the Comparison category.

T.PeakConcentration.note: The Transformation adds nothing. All dimensional relationships are inherited from the SequenceAggregate category.

inheritedFromConcept.Subject: partition (cardinality 1)
inheritedFromConcept.Parameter: partition — each input carries its own Parameter (WEIGHT, HEIGHT)

T.BMICalculation.note: Parameter is inherited and present on both inputs, but each input has a different parameter value. The output creates a new parameter (BMI).
